subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a thrice differentiable function such that $f(0)=0, f(1)=1, f(2)=-1, f(3)=2$ and $f(4)=-2$. Then, the minimum number of zeros of $\left(3 f^{\prime} f^{\prime \prime}+f f^{\prime \prime \prime}\right)(x)$ is _______
question: Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a thrice differentiable function such that $f(0)=0, f(1)=1, f(2)=-1, f(3)=2$ and $f(4)=-2$. Then, the minimum number of zeros of $\left(3 f^{\prime} f^{\prime \prime}+f f^{\prime \prime \prime}\right)(x)$ is _______
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \left(3 f^{\prime} f^{\prime \prime}+f f^{\prime \prime \prime}\right)(x)=\left(\left(f f^{\prime \prime}+\left(f^{\prime}\right)^2\right)(x)\right)^{\prime} \\ & \left(f f f^{\prime \prime}+\left(f^{\prime}\right)^2\right)(x)=\left(\left(f f^{\prime}\right)(x)\right)^{\prime} \\ & \therefore\left(3 f^{\prime} f^{\prime \prime}+f^{\prime \prime \prime}\right)(x)=\left(f(x) \cdot f^{\prime}(x)\right)^{\prime \prime}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \min \text { roots of } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \rightarrow 4 \\ & \therefore \min \text {. roots of } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \rightarrow 3 \\ & \therefore \min \text { roots of }\left(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right) \rightarrow 7 \\ & \therefore \min \text { roots of }\left(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right)^{\prime \prime} \rightarrow 5\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
The function fx=x3-6x2+ax+b is such that f2=f4=0. 

Consider two statements:

S1 there exists x1,x2∈2,4, x1<x2, such that f'x1=-1 and f'x2=0.

S2 there exists x3,x4∈2,4, x3<x4, such that f is decreasing in 2,x4, increasing in x4,4 and 2f'x3=3fx4 then
question:
The function fx=x3-6x2+ax+b is such that f2=f4=0. 

Consider two statements:

S1 there exists x1,x2∈2,4, x1<x2, such that f'x1=-1 and f'x2=0.

S2 there exists x3,x4∈2,4, x3<x4, such that f is decreasing in 2,x4, increasing in x4,4 and 2f'x3=3fx4 then
solution:
Given:

fx=x3-6x2+ax+b

Given that:

f2=0⇒2a+b=16    ....i

f4=0⇒4a+b=32    ....ii

Solving both equations, we get

a=8, b=0

∴  fx=x3-6x2+8x

⇒  fx=xx-2x-4

Now,

f'x=3x2-12x+8

Now,

If f'x=-1

⇒3x2-12x+8=-1

⇒3x2-12x+9=0

⇒x2-4x+3=0

⇒x2-3x-x+3=0

⇒x-1x-3=0

⇒x=1, 3

Since, x1∈2,4, therefore x1=3.

And,

f'x=0

⇒3x2-12x+8=0

⇒x=12±144-966

⇒x=12±486

⇒x=12±436=6±233

⇒x=2+233, 2-233

Then, 2+233∈2,4

Hence, x2=2+233

So, S1 is true.

Now,

f'x=x-2+233x-2-233

Sign scheme for f'x is as follows:

f'x>0 ∀x∈-∞,2-233∪2+233,∞, hence it is increasing.

f'x<0 ∀x∈2-233,2+233, hence it is decreasing.

So, x4=2+233.

Now,

2f'x3=3fx4

⇒23x32-12x3+8=32+2332+233-22+233-4

⇒23x32-12x3+8=3233+2233233-2

⇒23x32-12x3+8=2129-4

⇒3x32-12x3+8=-83

⇒9x32-36x3+32=0

⇒x3=36±1296-115218=36±1218

⇒x3=83, 43

So, S2 is true.

q: The equation of the normal to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
question: The equation of the normal to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
solution:
y=1+x2y+cos2sin-1x
Now at x=0, y=2

Let sin-1x=t ⇒sint=x

⇒cos2sin-1x=cos2t=1-sin2t=1-x2

y=1+x2y+1-x2

y=e2yln1+x+1-x2

dydx=e2yln(1+x)2y1+x+ln(1+x)·2dydx-2x

Now at x=0, dydx=2×21+0+0

dydx=4
y-2=-14x-0
4y-8=-x
x+4y=8

q: The shortest distance between the line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and the curve <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is
question: The shortest distance between the line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and the curve <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is
solution:
y2=x–2
Differentiating w.r.t. x we get,
2yy'= 1⇒y'=  12y
For the shortest distance, the tangent at point P  will be parallel to the given line
⇒y'|p=12y1=1⇒y1=12
⇒x1=2+122=94 (∵y12=x1-2)
The shortest distance between the given curve & the line
= The perpendicular distance of point P from the line
=94-1212+12=742=742

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of the abosolute maximum and minimum values of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to :
question: The sum of the abosolute maximum and minimum values of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to :
solution:
Given,

fx=x2-5x+6-3x+2

⇒fx=x-3x-2-3x+2

⇒fx=x2-8x+8;x∈-1,2-x2+2x-4;x∈(2,3]

⇒f'x=2x-8;x∈-1,2-x+2;x∈2,3

Now point of extrema will be 2x-8=0⇒x=4which is not in domain and -x+2=0⇒x=2

Now for finding the value of global minima and maxima we will check the value of function at extrema points and boundary points,

So, fx=x2-8x+8 will give,  f-1=17 & f2=-4

And fx=-x2+2x-4 will give, f2=4, f3=-7

Hence, absolute minima is -7 and maxima is 17

So, their sum is -7+17=10

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be a non constant twice differentiable such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>g</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>g</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>. If a real valued function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>[</mo><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>]</mo></math>, then
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be a non constant twice differentiable such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>g</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>g</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>. If a real valued function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>[</mo><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>]</mo></math>, then
solution:
Given: fx=12gx+g2-x

⇒f'x=12g'x-g'2-x

⇒f'12=12g'12-g'32

⇒f'12=0

Also, f'32=12g'32-g'12

⇒f'32=0

Now, f'1=12g'1-g'1

⇒f'1=0

So, f'x has three roots and thus f"x will have atleast two roots in 0,2

q: If the curves, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> touch each other at a point, then the largest value of <math><mi>k</mi></math> is ____________.
question: If the curves, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> touch each other at a point, then the largest value of <math><mi>k</mi></math> is ____________.
solution:
If general equation of circle is x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0 then its center is -g,-f and radius is g2+f2-c

Two circles touches each other if C1C2=r1±r2

Distance between C23,0andC10,4 is either  k+1ork-1

C1C2=3-02+0-42=5

⇒k+1=5or k+1=5  

⇒k=16 or k=36

⇒ Maximum value of k is 36.

subject: Mathematics
q: If a curve passes through the point $\left(2, \frac{7}{2}\right)$ and has slope $\left(1-\frac{1}{x^2}\right)$ at any point $(x, y)$ on it, then the ordinate of the point on the curve whose abscissa is $-2$ is :
question: If a curve passes through the point $\left(2, \frac{7}{2}\right)$ and has slope $\left(1-\frac{1}{x^2}\right)$ at any point $(x, y)$ on it, then the ordinate of the point on the curve whose abscissa is $-2$ is :
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Slope }=\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{1}{x^2} \\
& \Rightarrow \int d y=\int\left(1-\frac{1}{x^2}\right) d x \\
& \Rightarrow y=x+\frac{1}{x}+\mathrm{C}, \text { which is the equation of }
\end{aligned}
$$
the curve
since curve passes through the point
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(2, \frac{7}{2}\right) \\
\therefore \quad & \frac{7}{2}=2+\frac{1}{2}+C \Rightarrow C=1 \\
\therefore \quad & y=x+\frac{1}{x}+1
\end{aligned}
$$
when $x=-2$, then $y=-2+\frac{1}{-2}+1=\frac{-3}{2}$

q: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> be a polynomial of degree <math><mn>5</mn></math> such that <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>±</mo><mn>1</mn></math> are its critical points. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></munder><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then which one of the following is not true?
question: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> be a polynomial of degree <math><mn>5</mn></math> such that <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>±</mo><mn>1</mn></math> are its critical points. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></munder><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then which one of the following is not true?
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> . <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a point of maximum and <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi></mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>is a point of local minimum and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a point of local maximum
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a point of local maxima of <math><mi>f</mi></math>
solution:
fx=ax5+bx4+cx3

Consider, limx→0⁡2+ax5+bx4+cx3x3=4

⇒2+c=4

⇒c=2

Differentiating fx with respect to x

f'x=5ax4+4bx3+6x2

=x25ax2+4bx+6

Critical points of the function are given as ±1.

f'1=0⇒5a+4b+6=0

f'-1=0⇒5a-4b+6=0
Solving both we get, b=0, a=-65
So, fx=-65x5+2x3
And f'x=-6x4+6x2

=-6x2x+1x-1

Hence, local minima at x=-1 and local maxima at x=1.

subject: Mathematics
q: Given $P(x)=x^4+a x^3+b x^2+c x+d$ such that $x=0$ is the only real root of $P^{\prime}(x)=0$. If $P(-1) < P(1)$, then in the interval $[-1,1]$
question: Given $P(x)=x^4+a x^3+b x^2+c x+d$ such that $x=0$ is the only real root of $P^{\prime}(x)=0$. If $P(-1) < P(1)$, then in the interval $[-1,1]$
options: $P(-1)$ is the minimum and $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$
$P(-1)$ is not minimum but $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$
$P(-1)$ is the minimum and $P(1)$ is not the maximum of $P$
neither $P(-1)$ is the minimum nor $P(1)$ is the maximum of $P$
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P(x)=x^4+a x^3+b x^2+c x+d \\
& P^{\prime}(x)=4 x^3+3 a x^2+2 b x+c \\
& \because x=0 \text { is a solution for } P^{\prime}(x)=0, \Rightarrow c=0 \\
& \therefore P(x)=x^4+a x^3+b x^2+d
\end{aligned}
$$
Also, we have $P(-1) < P(1)$
$$
\Rightarrow 1-a+b+d < 1+a+b+d \Rightarrow a>0
$$
$\because P^{\prime}(x)=0$, only when $x=0$ and $P(x)$ is differentiable in $(-1,1)$, we should have the maximum and minimum at the points $x=-1,0$ and 1 only
Also, we have $P(-1) < P(1)$
$\therefore$ Max. of $P(x)=\operatorname{Max} .\{P(0), P(1)\}$ \& Min. of $P(x)=\operatorname{Min} .\{P(-1), P(0)\}$
In the interval $[0,1]$,
$$
P^{\prime}(x)=4 x^3+3 a x^2+2 b x=x\left(4 x^2+3 a x+2 b\right)
$$
$\because P^{\prime}(x)$ has only one root $x=0,4 x^2+3 a x+2 b=0$ has no real roots.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore(3 a)^2-32 b < 0 \Rightarrow \frac{3 a^2}{32} < b \\
& \therefore b>0
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, we have $a>0$ and $b>0$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=4 \mathrm{x}^3+3 a \mathrm{x}^2+2 \mathrm{bx}>0, \forall \mathrm{x} \in(0,1)
$$
Hence $P(x)$ is increasing in $[0,1]$
$\therefore$ Max. of $P(x)=P(1)$
Similarly, $P(x)$ is decreasing in $[-1,0]$
Therefore Min. $P(x)$ does not occur at $x=-1$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2 \mathrm{x}^2-9 a \mathrm{x}^2+12 \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{x}+1$, where $\mathrm{a}>0$, attains its maximum and minimum at $\mathrm{p}$ and $\mathrm{q}$ respectively such that $\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q}$, then a equals
question: If the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2 \mathrm{x}^2-9 a \mathrm{x}^2+12 \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{x}+1$, where $\mathrm{a}>0$, attains its maximum and minimum at $\mathrm{p}$ and $\mathrm{q}$ respectively such that $\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q}$, then a equals
solution:
$f(x)=2 x^3-9 a x^2+12 a^2 x+1$
$f^{\prime}(x)=6 x^2-18 a x+12 a^2 ; f^{\prime \prime}(x)=12 x-18 a$
For max. or min. $6 x^2-18 a x+12 a^2=0 \Rightarrow x^2-3 a x+2 a^2=0$
$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}$ or $\mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{a}$, at $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}$ max.and at $\mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{a} \min$.
$p^2=q$
$a^2=2 a \Rightarrow a=2$ or $a=0$
but $\mathrm{a}>0$, therefore, $\mathrm{a}=2$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then
solution:
Given:

fx=2x+tan-1x

⇒f'x=2+11+x2

And,

gx=ln1+x2+x

⇒g'x=11+x2

Now,

0≤x≤3

⇒0≤x2≤9

⇒1≤1+x2≤10

So,

110≤11+x2≤1

⇒2+110≤2+11+x2≤3

⇒2+110≤f'x≤3

⇒2110≤f'x≤3

And,

110≤g'x≤1

So, min f'x=2110≠1+max g'x

Option 4 is incorrect

From above,

g'x<f'x∀x∈0,3

Option 1 is incorrect.

Since, f'x and g'x are both positive so fx and gx both are increasing

So,

maxfx at x=3 is 6+tan-13

maxgx at x=3 is ln3+10 

And, 6+tan-13>ln3+10 

Option 2 is correct

q: The function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></math>:
question: The function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></math>:
solution:
fx=4x3-3x26-2sinx+2x-1cosx

f'x=2x2-x-2cosx+2cosx-sinx2x-1.

=2x-1x-sinx

for x>0,x-sinx>0

x<0,x-sinx<0

for x∈-∞,0∪12,∞,f'x≥0

for x∈0,12,f'x≤0

⇒ fx increases in 12,∞

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be a real polynomial of degree <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> which vanishes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> have local minima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, local maxima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>1</mn></msubsup><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>18</mn></math>, then the sum of all the coefficients of the polynomial <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to ___ .
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be a real polynomial of degree <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> which vanishes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> have local minima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, local maxima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>1</mn></msubsup><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>18</mn></math>, then the sum of all the coefficients of the polynomial <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to ___ .
solution:
Let P'(x)=a(x-1)(x+1)=ax2-1

Px=a∫x2-1dx+c

=ax33-x+c

Now P(-3)=0

⇒a-273+3+c=0

⇒-6a+c=0  …(1)

Now ∫-11ax33-x+cdx=18

=2c=18⇒c=9  ...2

 From (1) & (2) ⇒-6a+9=0⇒a=32

⇒Px=32x33-x+9

Then, the Sum of coefficient=12-32+9

=8

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of distinct real roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
question: The number of distinct real roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
solution:
Given,

x5x3-x2-x+1+x3x3-4x2-2x+4-1=0

Now on rearranging we get

⇒x8-x7-x6+x5+3x4-4x3-2x2+4x-1=0

⇒x7x-1-x5x-1+3x3x-1-xx2-1-2xx-1+x-1=0

⇒x-1x7-x5+3x3-xx+1-2x+1=0

⇒x-1x5x2-1+3xx2-1-1x2-1=0

⇒x2-1x-1x5+3x-1=0

From above equation it is clearly visible that the root are x=±1 and for x5+3x-1

Let fx=x5+3x-1, f'x=5x4+3

f'x>0∀x∈R, hence it is monotonic in nature so it will have only one root other than 1 & -1.

Hence 3 real distinct roots.

subject: Mathematics
q: The weight $W$ of a certain stock of fish is given by $W=n w$, where $n$ is the size of stock and $w$ is the average weight of a fish. If $n$ and $w$ change with time $t$ as $n=2 t^2+3$ and $w=t^2-t+2$, then the rate of change of $W$ with respect to $t$ at $t=1$ is
question: The weight $W$ of a certain stock of fish is given by $W=n w$, where $n$ is the size of stock and $w$ is the average weight of a fish. If $n$ and $w$ change with time $t$ as $n=2 t^2+3$ and $w=t^2-t+2$, then the rate of change of $W$ with respect to $t$ at $t=1$ is
solution:
Let $\mathrm{W}=n w$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{d W}{d t}=n \frac{d w}{d t}+w \cdot \frac{d n}{d t}
$$
Given : $w=t^2-t+2$ and $n=2 t^2+3$ $\Rightarrow \frac{d w}{d t}=2 t-1$ and $\frac{d n}{d t}=4 t$
$\therefore$ Equation (1)
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{d w}{d t}=\left(2 t^2+3\right)(2 t-1)+\left(t^2-t+2\right)(4 t)
$$
Thus, $\left.\frac{d W}{d t}\right|_{t=1}=(2+3)(2-1)+(2)(4)$
$$
=5(1)+8=13
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The shortest distance between the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the curve <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> , is
question: The shortest distance between the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the curve <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> , is
solution:
Let P be the nearest to 32, 0, then normal at P will pass through 32, 0.

Any point on the parabola y2=4ax can be taken as at2, 2at

Let, co-ordinates of P on the curve y=x, ⇒y2=x can be taken as t24, t2


The equation of the normal to the parabola y2=4ax at a point at2, 2at is y+tx=2at+at3.

Hence, the equation of normal to y=x or y2=x at t24, t2 is y+tx=t2+t34

This line passes through 32, 0, hence 
3t2=t2+t34

⇒t3-4t=0

⇒tt2-4=0

⇒t=0, 2, -2 but -2 is rejected as we have to take point in the first quadrant.

Thus, the points are 0, 0 or 1, 1 and the distances of these points from 32, 0 are respectively

32-02+0-02=32 and 32-12+1-02=52 units.

Hence, nearest point is 1,1 and the minimum distance is 52 units.

q: The shortest distance between the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></math> is:
question: The shortest distance between the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></math> is:
solution:
Shortest distance between curves is always along common normal.

Let Px0, y0 be any point on the parabola x2=2y.

Clearly, slope of line x-y=1 is 1. 

Hence, slope of perpendicular line is -1.

dydxP=Slope of tangent at P 

Therefore, -1dydxP=Slope of normal=-1

⇒dydxP=1

⇒x0=1 ∴y0=12

⇒P1,12

∴ Shortest distance =1-12-112+12=122

option 3

q: Let C be a curve given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is a point on C, such that the tangent at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> has slope <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, then a point through which the normal at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> passes, is :
question: Let C be a curve given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is a point on C, such that the tangent at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> has slope <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, then a point through which the normal at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> passes, is :
solution:
Given, 

yx=1+ 4x-3

So, dydx=124x-3 ×4=23

⇒ 4x-3=9

⇒ x=3

So, y=4

Equation of normal at  P3, 4 is y-4=-32x-3

i.e., 2y-8=-3x+9

⇒ 3x+2y-17=0

Hence only option A is satisfying the equation.

subject: Mathematics
q: The function $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$ is an increasing function in
question: The function $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$ is an increasing function in
solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}(\cos x-\sin x)$
$=\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}$
$f(x)$ is increasing if $-\frac{\pi}{2} < x+\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{\pi}{2}$
$-\frac{3 \pi}{4} < x < \frac{\pi}{4}$
hence $f(x)$ is increasing when $x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$.

question: Consider <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> A normal to <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> at <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> also passes through the point

at x=π6

f′x=122 .12 13 314=12

So slope of normal = -2

So equation of the Normal would be y-π3= -2 x-π6

It passes through 0,2π3

subject: Mathematics
q: If $f(x)$ is a quadratic expression such that $f(1)+f$ (2) $=0$, and $-1$ is a root of $f(x)=0$, then the other root of $f(x)=0$ is
question: If $f(x)$ is a quadratic expression such that $f(1)+f$ (2) $=0$, and $-1$ is a root of $f(x)=0$, then the other root of $f(x)=0$ is
solution:
If $a$ and $-1$ are the roots of the polynomial, then we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=x^2+(1-a) x-a . \\
\therefore \quad & f(1)=2-2 a \\
\text { and } & f(2)=6-3 a \\
\text { As, } & f(1)+f(2)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 2-2 a+6-3 a=0 \Rightarrow a=\frac{8}{5}
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore, the other root is $\frac{8}{5}$

q: Let the set of all values of $p$, for which $f(x)=\left(p^2-6 p+8\right)\left(\sin ^2 2 x-\cos ^2 2 x\right)+2(2-p) x+7$ does not have any critical point, be the interval $(a, b)$. Then $16 a b$ is equal to _______
question: Let the set of all values of $p$, for which $f(x)=\left(p^2-6 p+8\right)\left(\sin ^2 2 x-\cos ^2 2 x\right)+2(2-p) x+7$ does not have any critical point, be the interval $(a, b)$. Then $16 a b$ is equal to _______
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=-\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \cos 4 n+2(2-p) n+7 \\ & f^1(x)=+4\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \sin 4 x+(4-2 p) \neq 0 \\ & \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2 p-4}{4(p-4)(p-2)}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2(p-2)}{4(p-4)(p-2)} \\
& p \neq 2 \\
& \quad \sin 4 x \neq \frac{1}{2(p-4)} \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\frac{1}{2(p-4)}\right|>1
\end{aligned}$
on solving we get
$\therefore \mathrm{p} \in\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{9}{2}\right)$
Hence $\mathrm{a}=\frac{7}{2}, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{9}{2}$
$\therefore 16 \mathrm{ab}=252$

subject: Mathematics
q: $\max_{0 \leq x \leq \pi}\left(16\sin\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\right)$ is equal to:
question: $\max_{0 \leq x \leq \pi}\left(16\sin\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\right)$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $y = \dfrac{x}{2}$. Since $0 \leq x \leq \pi$, we have $0 \leq y \leq \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

The given expression becomes $f(y) = 16\sin y \cos^3 y$.

Differentiating with respect to $y$:

$f'(y) = 16(\cos y \cdot \cos^3 y + \sin y \cdot 3\cos^2 y(-\sin y))$

$f'(y) = 16\cos^2 y(\cos^2 y - 3\sin^2 y)$

For maximum value, $f'(y) = 0$:

$\cos^2 y - 3\sin^2 y = 0 \Rightarrow \tan^2 y = \dfrac{1}{3}$

Since $y \in \left[0, \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right]$, $\tan y = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

Substituting $y = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ in $f(y)$:

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 16\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 16 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^3$

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 8 \times \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} = 3\sqrt{3}$

The maximum value is $3\sqrt{3}$.

Answer: $3\sqrt{3}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The set of all a∈ℝ for which the equation x|x-1|+|x+2|+a=0 has exactly one real root, is
question: The set of all a∈ℝ for which the equation x|x-1|+|x+2|+a=0 has exactly one real root, is
solution:
Given,

x|x-1|+|x+2|+a=0

Now taking, Case I: x<-2, we get

-x2+x-x-2+a=0

⇒a=x2+2

And y=x2+2 is decreasing ∀x∈(-∞,-2)

Now taking Case II: -2≤x<1 we get,

-x2+x+x+2+a=0

⇒a=x2-2x-2

And y=x2-2x-2 

So, dydx=2x-1≤0 ∀ x∈[-2,1)

Hence, y is decreasing ∀x∈[-2,1)

Now taking Case III: x≥1 we get,

x2-x+x+2+a=0

⇒a=-x2+2

And y=-x2+2 is decreasing ∀x∈[1,∞)

Hence, from all the cases we can say that nature of function is continuously decreasing so, it will cut x-axis only one time,

∴ Exactly one real root ∀ a∈R

q: If $x$ is real, the maximum value of $\frac{3 x^2+9 x+17}{3 x^2+9 x+7}$ is
question: If $x$ is real, the maximum value of $\frac{3 x^2+9 x+17}{3 x^2+9 x+7}$ is
solution:
$y=\frac{3 x^2+9 x+17}{3 x^2+9 x+7}$
$3 x^2(y-1)+9 x(y-1)+7 y-17=0$
$D \geq 0 \quad \because x$ is real
$81(y-1)^2-4 x 3(y-1)(7 y-17) \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow(y-1)(y-41) \leq 0 \Rightarrow 1 \leq y \leq 41$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a critical point of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a critical point of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> is a local maxima of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a local maxima and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> is a local minima of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a local minima and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> are local maxima of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math>
solution:
f(x)=3x2+ax-2-aex
f'(x)=3x2+ax-2-aex+ex(6x+a)=ex3x2+(a+6)x-2

∵  x=1 is a critical point   ∴  f'(1)=0

∴3+a+6-2=0
a=-7

∴f'(x)=ex3x2-x-2=ex3x2-3x+2x-2=ex(3x+2)(x-1)

∴ maxima at x=-2 3∴ minima at x=1
